title: Reflection
text:
Module Checklist
A short checklist which demonstrates how this website has met the main requirements of this assessment:

Set up a Git repository and commit regularly
Deploy a Quarto website to GitHub Pages
Create all required pages (e.g About, Projects, Reflection)
Add a CV as a linked PDF
Create a projects listing with executable Python code
Customise the theme with SCSS
Implement a satisfactory number of markdown features
Implement Google Font
Maintain a clean, professional folder repository
Add all required social links

title: Reflection
text:
Critical Reflection

What I Found Challenging
To be honest, initially the entire assignment felt challenging. I was not very familiar with GitHub, and found it a little intimidating to be constantly pushing things not knowing what might happen. Of course, this was before I learned about rendering and previews. There were initially a number of occassions where I had to start from scratch, but each time I progressed further. Initially, implementing active Python code was also quite difficult, but I was able to overcome this over time.


What I Would Do Differently
If I was to start again, I would aim to have a more established plan and list of tasks that need to be done. Whilst the final assignment rubric helped a lot of formulating a plan, I should’ve had one set up myself beforehand as it would’ve prevented chaotic additions and then retractions to my portfolio.


How These Skills Apply Beyond This Module
The combination of Git, Python, and web publishing is directly transferable to data analyst and business intelligence roles. Being able to present analysis in a professional self-made portfolio, and to be familiar with GitHub is a necessary skill nowadays. An underrated part of this assignment is that it has also given us a GitHub commit history, which can be checked by some employers (particularly if I was to go into data analytics). I also became far more comfortable with using VS Code, software that is nowadays synonymous with programming. Although I mostly aim to further my education and perhaps pursue academia, the skills I have learned from this project will allow me to easily differentiate myself.

title: Reflection
text:
Future Goals
I plan to keep using this portfolio after graduating to differentiate myself in a tight job market, whilst adding to it as I plan my own projects outside of university. I aim to implement data analytics to whatever industry I enter, as I am a firm believer that having more information at your disposal is always beneficial, no matter the setting. I am genuinely grateful to have had the experience to complete this assessment, and believe it will serve me well in the future.

title: OLS Regression to Predict Median House Values
text:
This analysis uses multiple OLS regression to investigate the drivers of median house values across Philadelphia, finding that education levels and poverty rates were the strongest predictors. The model explained 66% of variance in house values, though spatial clustering in the data raised concerns about the independence assumption.






Note



This project was completed as part of “Spatial Econometrics and Data” at Waseda University, Japan.

title: Machine Learning Analysis on Company Bankruptcy
text:
This study applies four ML models (SVM, Random Forest, KNN, XGBoost) to a Taiwanese bankruptcy dataset, using SMOTE oversampling and SelectKBest feature selection to address class imbalance. XGBoost achieved the highest F2 score of 0.554, while SVM demonstrated the strongest recall, making it most suitable for real-world bankruptcy detection.






Note



This project was completed as part of BAA1027 Data Analytics: Machine Learning & Advanced Python at DCU.

title: Machine Learning Analysis on Company Bankruptcy
text:
Visualisations from Project
Below are some snippets of the Python code that was used during this project, focusing primarily on the code used to create visualisations to understand the dataset I was dealing with.

Shape of Dataset

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")

df.columns = df.columns.str.strip() #removes possible white space from columns that could break it

#describing the shape of the data
print("Shape of data:", df.shape) #displaying shape
print("No. of null values:", df.isnull().sum().sum()) #checking for null values
print("No. of duplicates:", df.duplicated().sum()) #checking for duplicates
print("\nClass:")
print(df["Bankrupt?" ].value_counts()) #checking class balance

Shape of data: (6819, 96)
No. of null values: 0
No. of duplicates: 0

Class:
Bankrupt?
0    6599
1     220
Name: count, dtype: int64




Class Distribution of Dataset

#as seen above, there is a large class imbalance
#this is why SMOTE will be needed later on to create artificial bankrupcties in data (oversampling)

counts = df["Bankrupt?"].value_counts()

plt.figure() #creating chart showing imbalanced class distribution
plt.bar(["Not Bankrupt", "Bankrupt"], [counts[0], counts[1]])
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Class Distribution")
plt.show()




Figure 1 - Class Distribution of the Taiwanese Bankruptcy Dataset






Correlation Heatmap of Dataset

import seaborn as sns
top_20_cor_feat = df.corr()["Bankrupt?"].abs().nlargest(21).index

#creating a heatmap of the top 20 most correlated features, both neagative and positive. we see its mostly associated with assets and liabilities
sns.heatmap(df[top_20_cor_feat].corr(), annot=False, linewidths=0.3)
plt.show()




Figure 2 - Correlation Heatmap

title: About Me
text:
I’m a fourth-year Business Studies International student at Dublin City University, specialising in the Japanese language and data analytics. I’m particularly interested in utilising data analytics to examine public policy and investment, which I have approached in some of my projects.
My goals for now involve continuing my education, particularly in relation to international relations, public policy and governance. I am particularly interested in applying quantitative methods of analysis onto these fields, which I would do by continuing to develop my skills with Python and SQL.



Languages: English (Native), Japanese (B2), Mandarin (A2)
Programming Languages: Python, CSS, SQL, HTML, R
Software: Power BI, GitHub, Microsoft Office, GeoDa, ArcGis Pro

title: Unique Characteristics of Ainu Culture
text:
A short Japanese-language essay examining the distinct cultural, linguistic, and ceremonial traditions of the Ainu people, the indigenous inhabitants of Hokkaido. It also addresses the marginalisation of the Ainu language and critiques the limitations of Japan’s 2019 recognition law.






Note



This project was completed as part of JPN1015 Perspectives on Japanese Culture at DCU.

title: Strategic Analysis of ABP Ireland
text:
A five-stage consulting analysis of ABP Ireland, Ireland’s largest beef processor, examining how the firm generates and sustains competitive advantage in a rapidly evolving agribusiness environment. The report recommends a strategic roadmap centred on three pillars: sustainability leadership, product diversification, and deeper vertical integration.






Note



This project was completed as part of BAA1019 Business Strategy at DCU.

title: Using Spatial Lag and Regression to Predict Income Inequality
text:
This report models the Gini index across New York City census tracts using OLS, spatial lag, spatial error, and geographically weighted regression to account for spatial autocorrelation. GWR performed best overall, explaining 57% of inequality variation with no residual spatial autocorrelation, outperforming all other models.






Note



This project was completed as part of “Spatial Econometrics and Data” at Waseda University, Japan.

title: Spatial Distribution of Farmers Markets in Philadelphia
text:
Using Nearest Neighbour Analysis and Ripley’s K-Function, this study examines whether farmers markets in Philadelphia are randomly distributed. Results confirm significant spatial clustering in central and western neighbourhoods, with northern and southern areas largely underserved, likely driven by population density rather than income differences.






Note



This project was completed as part of “Spatial Econometrics and Data” at Waseda University, Japan.
